input_candidates.truth_value: This study investigates the effects of Ti and V additions on κ-carbide precipitation behavior and mechanical properties in Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel, proposing a strategy using (Ti,V)C carbides as carbon sinks to control carbon distribution and optimize strength and work-hardening.

input_candidates.truth_value: Fig. 1 shows optical micrographs of M1 and M2 specimens; all exhibit equiaxed grain morphology. Grain sizes: M1-SS 42.4±7.8 μm, M1-30 45.3±9.3 μm, M2-SS 32.3±10.4 μm, M2-30 35.8±12.7 μm.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 10 tabulates strengthening mechanism contributions to yield strength: M1-SS measured 430 MPa (σ0=350, σG=80), M1-30 measured 860 MPa (σ0=350, σG=90, σS=430), M2-SS measured 660 MPa (σ0=410, σG=100, σOr=120), M2-30 measured 880 MPa (σ0=410, σG=100, σOr=120, σS=220). These tabulated values show minor discrepancies with narrative statements (M2-SS described as ~200 MPa higher than M1-SS; M2-30 stated as 884 MPa).

input_candidates.truth_value: Fig. 2: EBSD IPF maps indicate no significant texture in M2-SS and M2-30; grain size distribution evolves from bimodal to unimodal after aging.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 7 presents engineering stress-strain and true stress-strain curves with strain hardening rate curves for M1-SS, M1-30, M2-SS, and M2-30; specific UTS and elongation values for M1-SS and M2-SS are not explicitly stated in the text.

input_candidates.truth_value: Fig. 3: TEM BF images show no precipitates in M1-SS, while M2-SS contains (Ti,V)C carbides with size 35.8±20.1 nm.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 11 presents Crussard-Jaoul work-hardening index plots (ln(dσ/dε) vs lnε) showing three stages for M1-SS and two stages for M1-30, whereas M2-SS and M2-30 curves nearly coincide, indicating that the uniform precipitation of κ-carbides reduces work-hardening capacity while Ti/V modification preserves it.

input_candidates.truth_value: Fig. 4: TEM micrographs of precipitates; κ-carbides in M1-30 are uniformly distributed with number density ~7×10^22 m−3; in M2-10 and M2-30, κ preferentially precipitates at (Ti,V)C edges.
input_candidates.prediction_value: In M1-30, TEM DF imaging revealed a number density of uniformly distributed κ-carbides of approximately 7×10^22 m^-3.

input_candidates.truth_value: Fig. 5: TEM-EDS mapping of M2-30 shows V enrichment in (Ti,V)C; no Mn or Al enrichment in κ-carbides, suggesting ordering mechanism rather than compositional partitioning.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Calculated stacking fault energies are approximately 70 mJ/m^2 for M1 and 78 mJ/m^2 for M2 based on the Olson-Cohen model, indicating dislocation slip as the predominant deformation mode.

input_candidates.truth_value: Fig. 6: HRTEM shows intergrowth of κ-carbide and (Ti,V)C with orientation relationship (002)(Ti,V)C || (001)κ; κ-carbides appear linearly aligned along (Ti,V)C edges.
input_candidates.prediction_value: The tensile strength increased from 1004 MPa (M1-30) to 1096 MPa (M2-30), but the text does not express this improvement as a percentage.

input_candidates.truth_value: Fig. 7: Tensile curves; M1-SS YS ~430 MPa, M1-30 YS 860 MPa, elongation 40%, work-hardening rate drops; M2-SS YS ~660 MPa, M2-30 YS 884 MPa, UTS 1096 MPa, work-hardening rate maintained.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 9 schematically illustrates the microstructural evolution in M2 steel: preexisting (Ti,V)C carbides are surrounded by carbon-enriched zones, and upon aging at 550°C κ-carbides preferentially precipitate along the edges of (Ti,V)C carbides.
